intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

body.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

draft.title: recipe app for people who hate cooking
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
recipe app for people who hate cooking

Building your first draft…

data.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking want moments of playful feedback and charm that make the app feel alive, not just functional
facade.axisTargeted: Warm vs cool — emotional temperature of interaction feedback
facade.label: Micro-interactions delight
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into celebratory micro-animations, satisfying button states, and personality-driven moments that reward user actions
facade.rejectImplies: Strip to essentials — invisible interactions, minimal feedback, prioritize speed and clarity over charm

facade.hypothesis: Users hate cooking partly because ingredient prep feels tedious—a fast, fluid card-flip through ingredients (instead of a static checklist) makes the process feel like play, not work.
facade.axisTargeted: MOTION AND BEHAVIOR — animated swipe-through vs. static list
facade.label: Ingredient swipe-through
facade.acceptImplies: The app should use gesture-based, card-like patterns for ingredient selection and prep steps—each action flows into the next with momentum.
facade.rejectImplies: Stick to traditional static lists, checkboxes, and linear step-by-step navigation—users don't need (or want) motion to feel engaged with ingredient prep.

facade.hypothesis: A recipe app for people who hate cooking should feel informal and anti-perfectionist—handwritten type signals 'this doesn't have to be fancy' and lowers the stakes
facade.label: Messy handwritten fonts
facade.acceptImplies: The app embraces a casual, doodle-like aesthetic that makes cooking feel less intimidating and more personal
facade.rejectImplies: The app needs a cleaner, more polished visual system—users want clarity and trust, not quirky informality

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking want minimal friction—show complete recipes in a single card with just the essentials (ingredients, time, steps), no navigation or scrolling required.
facade.axisTargeted: Dense vs spacious; cards vs lists
facade.label: One-tap recipe
facade.acceptImplies: Users prioritize speed and simplicity over exploration or detailed information architecture.
facade.rejectImplies: Users want to browse, compare, or have recipes broken into digestible sections across multiple screens.

evidence.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking want moments of playful feedback and charm that make the app feel alive, not just functional
evidence.implication: Lean into celebratory micro-animations, satisfying button states, and personality-driven moments that reward user actions

facade.hypothesis: A voice that's irreverent and conspiratorial—almost making fun of cooking itself—will resonate better with people who hate cooking than a neutral, encouraging tone
facade.axisTargeted: friendly vs professional + playful vs serious
facade.label: Snarky little helper
facade.acceptImplies: The app should have a witty, slightly sarcastic personality. Think: joking about how bad your knife skills are, roasting recipe complexity, celebrating shortcuts. Branded, memorable voice.
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer a more straightforward, encouraging, or neutral tone. The app should feel helpful and competent without trying to be funny or forming a 'buddy' relationship.

axes.label: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
Whether user wants minimal information (just do this) vs. detailed explanations (why this works, what you're learning)
axes.poleA: Just tell me or explain why

axes.label: Whether user prefers instant gratification with no waiting vs. enjoying a slower reveal and anticipation of results

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants calm, meditative cooking experience vs. quick-win, dopamine-hit satisfaction from easy wins
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants minimal information (just do this) vs. detailed explanations (why this works, what you're learning)
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers instant gratification with no waiting vs. enjoying a slower reveal and anticipation of results
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: People who hate cooking prefer step-by-step task lists over narrative recipe prose—breaking prep into discrete, completable actions reduces cognitive load and decision fatigue.
facade.axisTargeted: Information density and format: dense instructional prose vs. scannable, actionable lists
facade.label: Recipe as checklist
facade.acceptImplies: User sees recipes as *processes to execute* rather than stories to read. Design should favor checkboxes, numbered steps, timers, and ingredient checkoffs over narrative description and technique explanation.
facade.rejectImplies: User may want context, technique tips, and ingredient rationale *alongside* instructions—meaning recipe format should blend narrative explanation with structured steps, not strip it down to pure task execution.

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking feel intimidated by clinical, sterile recipe interfaces—warm, diffused light and golden tones create a forgiving, low-pressure atmosphere that makes the app feel like a supportive friend, not a demanding teacher
facade.label: Soft warm lighting
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into amber backgrounds, soft shadows, and a gentle color palette that feels intimate and encouraging rather than sharp or professional
facade.rejectImplies: Users don't need emotional warmth from the interface—they need clarity and speed; stay with bright, neutral, high-contrast design

draft.title: QuickBite – Recipe App for People Who Hate Cooking
draft.summary: A recipe app that strips away complexity and delivers only the fastest, easiest meals with minimal ingredients and steps. Perfect for busy people who want food without the fuss.
draft.html:
QuickBite

Meals in 15 minutes or less. No fuss.

Filter by Time

Today's Quick Meals

Pasta Aglio e Olio

Garlic, oil, pasta. Done.

8 min

Egg Fried Rice

Rice, eggs, soy sauce.

10 min

Avocado Toast

Bread, avocado, salt.

3 min

Tuna Salad

Canned tuna, greens, dressing.

5 min
